request.prompt: Build a CI/CD pipeline for a Next.js app with GitHub Actions, testing, staging deploy, and production release

response.message: Mission received. Building CI/CD pipeline for Next.js app with GitHub Actions, testing, staging deploy, and production release.

nodes.label: Code Push
nodes.description: Trigger on push to main or pull request to main branch.
nodes.content:
GitHub Actions trigger: on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]

nodes.label: PRD & Config
nodes.description: Requirements and configuration files.
nodes.content: Project Requirements Document (PRD) and configuration files (next.config.js, package.json, .github/workflows/ci-cd.yml, environment variables).

nodes.label: Build & Lint
nodes.description: Install dependencies, run linter, and build the Next.js application.
nodes.content:
GitHub Actions job:
- name: Build and Lint
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    - run: npm ci
    - run: npm run lint
    - run: npm run build

nodes.label: Unit & Integration Tests
nodes.description: Execute Jest/React Testing Library unit and integration tests.
nodes.content:
GitHub Actions job:
- name: Run Tests
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    - run: npm ci
    - run: npm test -- --coverage --passWithNoTests

nodes.label: Staging Deployment
nodes.description: Deploy the built application to a staging environment (e.g., Vercel Preview, AWS Amplify).
nodes.content:
GitHub Actions job (conditional on main branch push):
- name: Deploy to Staging
  if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  env:
    VERCEL_ORG_ID: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_ORG_ID }}
    VERCEL_PROJECT_ID: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_PROJECT_ID }}
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: amondnet/vercel-action@v25
      with:
        vercel-token: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}
        vercel-args: '--prod'

nodes.label: Staging Review
nodes.description: Manual approval gate for promotion to production.
nodes.content: Manual approval step in GitHub Actions workflow or external tool (e.g., Vercel Dashboard, manual check). Requires sign-off from QA/Product.

nodes.label: Production Release
nodes.description: Deploy the approved build to production environment.
nodes.content:
GitHub Actions job (triggered after staging approval):
- name: Deploy to Production
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  env:
    VERCEL_ORG_ID: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_ORG_ID }}
    VERCEL_PROJECT_ID: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_PROJECT_ID }}
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: amondnet/vercel-action@v25
      with:
        vercel-token: ${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}
        vercel-args: '--prod'

nodes.label: Pipeline Monitor
nodes.description: Monitor pipeline health, test coverage, and deployment status.
nodes.content: Dashboards and alerts (GitHub Actions status, Vercel Analytics, Sentry for errors, test coverage reports).

nodes.label: Deployed Artifacts
nodes.description: Final deployed application URLs and build artifacts.
nodes.content:
Production URL: https://myapp.vercel.app
Staging URL: https://myapp-git-main-myteam.vercel.app
Build logs and test reports stored in GitHub Actions artifacts.

request.prompt: Create a content publishing workflow: draft article, AI review, editor approval, SEO optimization, publish to CMS, social media distribution

response.message: Mission received. Building content publishing workflow with draft, review, approval, SEO, CMS publish, and social distribution.

nodes.label: Article Draft
nodes.description: Initial draft of the article content
nodes.content: Draft article content in markdown format. Includes title, body, images, and metadata.

nodes.label: AI Content Review
nodes.description: Automated review for grammar, tone, and basic compliance
nodes.content: Run AI review using tool (e.g., Grammarly, Writer). Check for clarity, grammar, brand voice, and flag potential issues.

nodes.label: Editor Approval
nodes.description: Human editor review and sign-off
nodes.content: Editor reviews AI feedback and draft. Makes final content and structural decisions. Gives approval to proceed.

nodes.label: SEO Optimization
nodes.description: Optimize article for search engines
nodes.content: Add target keywords, meta title/description, alt text, internal links, and URL slug. Ensure readability score.

nodes.label: CMS Publishing
nodes.description: Publish the finalized article to the Content Management System
nodes.content: Push article to CMS (e.g., WordPress, Contentful). Schedule publish time, set categories/tags, and final preview.

nodes.label: Social Media Distribution
nodes.description: Create and schedule social media posts for the published article
nodes.content: Generate social posts (LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook) with snippets, links, and images. Schedule via tool (e.g., Hootsuite, Buffer).

nodes.label: Publishing Trigger
nodes.description: Manual or scheduled trigger to start the workflow
nodes.content: Editor or schedule initiates the workflow for a new article.

checks: ✗ Missing "input" category (found: artifact, review, action, trigger)
✗ Missing "output" category (found: artifact, review, action, trigger)

request.prompt: Design an incident response workflow: alert detection, triage, investigation, mitigation, post-mortem, and preventive action

response.message: Aha! Mon ami, the case of the digital disturbance! The little grey cells are tingling. An incident response workflow—a systematic investigation to unmask the culprit, contain the chaos, and ensure it never dares to show its face again. Très intéressant! I shall design a procedural masterpiece, a blueprint for order in the face of operational calamity. Observe, the nodes and edges of our investigation are laid bare. Voilà!

nodes.label: Alert Ingest & Detection
nodes.description: Initial signal of a potential incident from monitoring tools, user reports, or automated systems.
nodes.content:
**Source:** SIEM alerts, APM thresholds, user support tickets, external threat intel feeds.
**Detection Criteria:** Unusual error rate spike (>5x baseline), failed login attempts from anomalous geolocation, critical service health check failure, malware signature match.

nodes.label: Initial Triage & Severity Assessment
nodes.description: Rapid evaluation to confirm incident, assign initial severity, and notify relevant responders.
nodes.content:
**Process:**
1. Confirm alert is not a false positive.
2. Assess impact scope (users, systems, data).
3. Assign Severity Level (SEV-0: Full outage, SEV-1: Major degradation, SEV-2: Minor issue, SEV-3: Informational).
4. Activate incident response channel (e.g., Slack #incident-war-room, PagerDuty).
**Output:** Declared incident with SEV level, initial impact statement.

nodes.label: Investigation War Room
nodes.description: Central hub for collaboration, evidence gathering, and timeline reconstruction.
nodes.content:
**Artifacts to Collect:**
- Logs from affected services (last 72 hours).
- System metrics (CPU, memory, network I/O).
- Recent deployment records and change tickets.
- User session data (if applicable).
**Tooling:** Shared document for timeline, evidence locker (S3 bucket), collaborative chat/voice channel.

nodes.label: Containment & Mitigation Actions
nodes.description: Execute short-term fixes to isolate the issue and restore service.
nodes.content:
**Potential Actions:**
- Block malicious IPs at firewall/WAF.
- Rollback recent deployment to last known good state.
- Failover traffic to healthy region/instance.
- Restart crashed service with increased resources.
- Apply emergency security patch.
**Rule:** Document every action taken for the post-mortem.

nodes.label: Service Restoration Verification
nodes.description: Validate that mitigation actions have successfully restored normal operation.
nodes.content:
**Verification Steps:**
1. Monitor key business metrics (error rate, latency, throughput) return to baseline.
2. Execute synthetic transactions/canary tests.
3. Confirm user-facing functionality is operational.
4. Obtain stakeholder sign-off (e.g., Product/Support lead).
**Exit Criteria:** All critical systems green for 15 consecutive minutes.

nodes.label: Post-Mortem (Blameless Analysis)
nodes.description: Structured retrospective to understand root cause, contributing factors, and lessons learned.
nodes.content:
**Agenda:**
1. Timeline reconstruction (from first symptom to resolution).
2. Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys, Fishbone diagram).
3. Identify what went well and what didn't (detection, communication, mitigation).
4. Draft actionable follow-up items.
**Output:** Published post-mortem document with timeline, root cause, and action items.

nodes.label: Preventive Action & Follow-up
nodes.description: Implement changes to prevent recurrence and improve the response system itself.
nodes.content:
**Action Item Examples:**
- **Technical:** Fix bug, improve monitoring coverage, add circuit breakers, update runbooks.
- **Process:** Revise escalation procedures, improve alert fatigue, schedule incident response drills.
- **Documentation:** Update architecture diagrams, create new playbook for this incident type.
**Ownership:** Each action item assigned to an owner and ETA. Tracked in project management tool.

nodes.label: Closed Incident & Knowledge Base Update
nodes.description: Final state. Incident record is closed, and insights are institutionalized.
nodes.content:
**Final Steps:**
1. Update incident tracking system status to 'Resolved'.
2. Archive all war room artifacts and communications.
3. Publish sanitized post-mortem to internal knowledge base.
4. Share key learnings in company-wide newsletter or tech talk.
**Goal:** Transform incident pain into organizational resilience.

checks: ✗ Missing "state" category (found: trigger, action, artifact, test, review, policy, output)

request.prompt: What makes a good deployment pipeline?

response.message: On it. A good deployment pipeline is automated, reliable, fast, and secure. Key elements: version control integration, automated testing, environment consistency, rollback capability, monitoring, and security scanning. It should enable frequent, low-risk deployments.

request.prompt: How should I structure a code review process?

response.message: Ah, mon ami, a most excellent question! The structure of a code review process is like a well-orchestrated investigation—each step must be deliberate, each clue examined. Let us apply the little grey cells. A robust code review workflow should include: a trigger (like a pull request), an initial automated check (linting, tests), a human review phase with clear criteria, a feedback loop for revisions, and a final approval gate before merging. It should also capture artifacts like review comments and metrics for continuous improvement. Would you like me to design a specific workflow for you? Just say the word, and I shall build it—voilà!

request.prompt: I need to onboard new employees

nodes.label: New Hire Trigger
nodes.description: HR system event or manual trigger for new employee start date.
nodes.content: Trigger: New employee record created in HRIS (e.g., Workday, BambooHR) with start date confirmed. Event payload includes employee ID, name, role, department, manager, start date.

nodes.label: Onboarding Policy
nodes.description: Company onboarding requirements and compliance checklist.
nodes.content: Policy Document: Mandatory steps: 1. IT provisioning (laptop, email, access). 2. Security & compliance training. 3. Role-specific tool access. 4. Manager 1:1 scheduled. 5. First-week goals set. 6. Benefits enrollment. 7. Office access/badge. Compliance: Complete I-9, tax forms, NDAs.

nodes.label: IT Provisioning
nodes.description: Automated IT ticket to provision hardware, software, accounts.
nodes.content: Action: Create Jira/ServiceNow ticket for IT. Auto-generate email account (GSuite/Office 365). Ship laptop/equipment. Provision Slack, VPN, internal tool access based on role. Assign to IT queue.

nodes.label: Training & Docs
nodes.description: Central repository of onboarding materials and training modules.
nodes.content: Artifact: Notion/Confluence page: Welcome guide, org chart, key contacts. Required training: Security awareness (KnowBe4), anti-harassment, product overview. Links to HR policies, expense reporting, time-off system.

nodes.label: Manager Checklist
nodes.description: Track manager-led onboarding tasks and first-week goals.
nodes.content: State: Manager tasks: Schedule day-1 intro meetings. Set up 30/60/90-day plan. Assign buddy/mentor. Review team norms. Confirm equipment received. Status: pending/in-progress/complete.

nodes.label: Compliance Review
nodes.description: HR review of signed documents and training completion.
nodes.content: Review: HR validates: I-9, W-4, direct deposit, signed NDA, completed training certificates. Escalate missing items after 3 days. Mark employee as 'compliant' in system.

nodes.label: Onboarding Complete
nodes.description: Final status and welcome notification.
nodes.content: Output: Onboarding checklist 100% complete. Send welcome email to team. Activate full system access. Log completion in HRIS. Trigger 30-day check-in survey.

request.prompt: Tell me about the current state of this workflow

response.message: Ah, mon ami! I have examined the scene with the utmost care. The current state of this workflow is... empty! There are no nodes, no edges—a blank canvas, a fresh mystery waiting to be solved. It is like a detective's notebook before the first clue is jotted down. Très intéressant! If you wish to build a new workflow, I am ready to assist. Just say the word, and we shall construct a case—er, a workflow—together. Parfait!
